skill.name: audit-website
skill.description: Audit websites for SEO, technical quality, accessibility, performance, content, links, and security issues with the squirrelscan CLI.
skill.summary: Run squirrelscan website audits and receive compact reports for SEO, technical, content, accessibility, performance, and security issues.
skill.author: squirrelscan
skill.license: See LICENSE file in repository root

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mostly documentation false positives from Markdown code fences, XML examples, URLs, and words such as SHA or sitemap. Human review confirmed high risk from remote shell installer instructions and broad third-party CLI execution, but found no prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Remote Shell Installer Recommended
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill tells users to install the CLI by downloading a script from an external website and piping it directly to a shell. This pattern can execute arbitrary remote code before the user can inspect it.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is explicit and appears in both installation and troubleshooting instructions. The intent is installation rather than malware, so this is high risk but not confirmed malicious.

high_findings.title: PowerShell Remote Script Execution Recommended
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The Windows install path downloads a remote PowerShell script and executes it immediately. This creates a supply-chain execution risk for users who follow the skill instructions.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command combines remote retrieval with immediate PowerShell execution. The surrounding text is normal installer documentation, which lowers confidence of malicious intent but not execution risk.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party CLI Crawl Runs Against User Targets
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill directs agents to run squirrel audit commands against live or local websites. This requires network crawling and may contact external links discovered during the audit.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The website crawling behavior is central to the skill and is clearly documented. It is legitimate for this skill but still creates network and privacy risk.

medium_findings.title: Local Environment Discovery Suggested
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The skill tells the agent to infer audit targets from the local directory and environment variables. This could expose deployment metadata or private local endpoints while choosing a target.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction explicitly mentions environment variables and local project discovery. No exfiltration path is shown, but the behavior needs user consent boundaries.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Flagged As Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The many backtick findings are Markdown formatting and command examples, not Ruby execution in source code. No executable Ruby file or dynamic backtick execution was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The affected files are documentation files and the backticks delimit Markdown or XML examples. There is no application code that evaluates these strings.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Keywords Are Documentation Terms
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography alerts are caused by words inside SEO, XML, and reporting documentation. No cryptographic implementation, hashing routine, or security control is defined by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations describe website audit features and XML escaping. They do not select or implement a cryptographic algorithm.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Product And Example References
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs point to the vendor website, documentation, or examples used for audit demonstrations. No evidence found that secrets or local data are sent to an unauthorized endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links and sample audit targets. The only real network concern is expected crawling, which is captured separately.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Paths Are Installation Locations
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hidden path findings document install directories, PATH setup, and project storage locations. No evidence found that the skill reads hidden files or collects secrets from them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are explanatory installation and troubleshooting text. They do not instruct the agent to read sensitive hidden files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Downloaded Installer To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a curl command that downloads an installer and pipes it to bash. This should be replaced with checksum verification and an inspect-before-run workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact installer flow is documented in executable command examples. This is a well-known supply-chain risk even when the vendor is legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Execute Remote PowerShell Installer
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a PowerShell command that retrieves a remote script and executes it immediately. Users should download, inspect, and verify the installer first.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The remote script execution pattern is explicit. The risk is high because it grants code execution to remote installer content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automated Website Crawling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill runs a crawler against supplied or discovered websites and checks external links. This can generate outbound traffic and process third-party page content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The crawl behavior is documented as core functionality. It is expected, but users should approve targets before execution.

content.value_statement: Website audits often scatter issues across SEO tools, crawlers, and manual checks. This skill runs squirrelscan and summarizes prioritized fixes for AI-assisted workflows.
actual_capabilities: Runs squirrel audit against a supplied website URL.
Reports health scores, category scores, failed checks, and warnings.
Finds broken links, meta tag issues, structured data issues, and content problems.
Checks performance, accessibility, mobile, crawlability, and security-related website signals.
Exports compact LLM-oriented reports through squirrel report with the llm format.
Supports refresh, resume, max page limits, verbose mode, and saved audit reports.
limitations: Requires the squirrel CLI to be installed and available in PATH.
Crawls target websites and may contact discovered external links.
Audit quality depends on target availability, crawl limits, and rendered page behavior.
Installer examples use remote shell execution and require strong user trust.

use_cases.title: Developer Pre-Release Check
use_cases.description: Run a focused audit before release to catch broken links, missing metadata, accessibility issues, and technical SEO regressions.

use_cases.title: SEO Remediation Planning
use_cases.description: Generate a prioritized issue list with affected URLs, rule categories, and suggested fixes for content and technical SEO work.

use_cases.title: AI-Assisted Site Maintenance
use_cases.description: Use Claude, Codex, or Claude Code to interpret squirrelscan output and plan safe local code changes.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Basic Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit https://example.com with squirrelscan and summarize the most important SEO, accessibility, and security issues.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Local Preview
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my local preview URL after I confirm the target. Focus on broken links, metadata, mobile behavior, and accessibility.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Fixes from Results
prompt_templates.scenario: Turn audit output into engineering tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a squirrelscan LLM report, group issues by priority, and propose code changes for the top five confirmed problems.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Before and After
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a fresh squirrelscan audit, compare it with the previous report, and identify regressions, resolved issues, and remaining blockers.

output_examples.input: Audit a marketing site before launch.
output: Overall site health score with the weakest categories highlighted.
Prioritized broken links, missing titles, missing descriptions, and accessibility issues.
Clear next actions for developers and content owners.

output_examples.input: Review a blog after a redesign.
output: Summary of changed crawl results and newly detected technical SEO problems.
List of affected URLs for duplicate metadata, redirect chains, and image issues.
Recommended fixes ordered by impact and effort.
Findings for HTTPS usage, mixed content, security headers, and leaked secret patterns.
Context for each issue and the pages where it appears.

best_practices: Confirm the exact target URL before running any crawl.
Use the LLM report format when asking an AI assistant to plan fixes.
Review installer scripts and verify binaries before following remote install commands.
anti_patterns: Do not crawl private, third-party, or production sites without authorization.
Do not pipe remote installers into a shell without inspection and verification.
Do not treat automated audit results as proof that a website is fully secure.

faq.question: What does this skill audit?
faq.answer: It audits SEO, technical quality, content, accessibility, performance, links, mobile behavior, crawlability, and security-related website signals.

faq.question: Does it require an external tool?
faq.answer: Yes. The squirrel CLI must be installed and available in PATH before the skill can run audits.

faq.question: Can it audit local development sites?
faq.answer: Yes, if the local site is running and the user confirms the target URL.

faq.question: Will it make network requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It crawls the target website and may check links found during the crawl.

faq.question: Is the LLM output format required?
faq.answer: It is recommended for AI assistants because it is compact and structured for issue analysis.

faq.question: Can it automatically fix every issue?
faq.answer: No. It reports issues and recommendations. Code changes still need user review and project-specific validation.
